stem: What is {{n}} rounded to the nearest ten?
solutionStandard: Look at the units digit; round up if it's 5 or more: {{n}} rounds to \({{Math.round(n/10)*10}}\).

stem: What is 6.47 rounded to the nearest tenth?
solutionStandard: The hundredths digit is 7, so round the tenths digit up: \(6.47 \to 6.5\).

stem: Estimate \(297 \times 41\) by rounding each number to the nearest ten, then compute the estimate exactly.
Which of the following is the best estimate of \(\dfrac{5978}{19.8}\)?
\(x = 4.999\) rounded to the nearest whole number.
solutionStandard: \(4.999\) rounds up to \(5\) (the tenths digit is 9). The quantities are equal.

stem: A store's revenue was $48,750 last month. Which of the following is the revenue rounded to the nearest thousand?
solutionStandard: The hundreds digit is 7 (\(\ge5\)), so round the thousands digit up: \(48{,}750 \to 49{,}000\).

stem: A survey estimates a population count by rounding to the nearest hundred. If the rounded value is 3,600, what is the smallest possible actual population?
solutionStandard: The smallest value that rounds up to 3,600 is 3,550 (values from 3,550 up to but not including 3,650 round to 3,600).

stem: Which of the following round to 15.0 when rounded to the nearest tenth? Select all that apply.
solutionStandard: Rounding to the nearest tenth means the hundredths digit determines rounding: \(14.96 \to 15.0\), \(15.04 \to 15.0\). But \(14.94 \to 14.9\) and \(15.06 \to 15.1\).

stem: Which of the following is the best estimate of \(\sqrt{50} \times 11\)?
solutionStandard: \(\sqrt{50} \approx 7.07\), so \(\sqrt{50}\times11 \approx 7 \times 11 = 77\), closest to 78.

stem: \(n\) rounds to 200 when rounded to the nearest hundred, and \(n\) is an integer.
solutionStandard: Values from 150 up to and including 249 round to 200 (250 would round up to 300). The greatest possible integer value is 249, equal to Quantity B.

stem: What is {{n}} rounded to the nearest hundred?
solutionStandard: Look at the tens digit; round up if it's 5 or more: {{n}} rounds to \({{Math.round(n/100)*100}}\).

stem: What is 3.14159 rounded to the nearest hundredth?
solutionStandard: The thousandths digit is 1 (less than 5), so the hundredths digit stays the same: \(3.14159 \to 3.14\).

stem: Estimate \(512 \times 29\) by rounding each number to the nearest ten, then compute the estimate exactly.
Which of the following is the best estimate of \(803 \div 39\)?
solutionStandard: Round to \(800 \div 40 = 20\).

stem: \(x = 125\), rounded to the nearest ten.
solutionStandard: 125 is exactly halfway between 120 and 130; by the standard "round half up" convention, it rounds to 130. The quantities are equal.

stem: Which of the following round to 20 when rounded to the nearest ten? Select all that apply.
solutionStandard: \(15, 24,\) and \(16\) all round to 20. \(25\) rounds up to 30 (halfway rounds up), and \(14\) rounds down to 10.

stem: A number rounds to 500 when rounded to the nearest hundred. What is the smallest possible integer value of the number?
solutionStandard: Values from 450 up to (but not including) 550 round to 500. The smallest possible integer value is 450.

stem: The population of a town, rounded to the nearest thousand, is 24,000. Which of the following could NOT be the actual population?
solutionStandard: Values from 23,500 up to (but not including) 24,500 round to 24,000. 24,500 is exactly halfway to 25,000 and rounds up to 25,000, so it could NOT be the actual population.

stem: A length of {{n}} cm is recorded, rounded to the nearest centimeter. What is the maximum possible difference, in cm, between the recorded value and the true length?
solutionStandard: Rounding to the nearest whole unit can shift a value by at most half of that unit, regardless of the value itself — here, at most 0.5 cm.

stem: A number \(x\) is rounded to the nearest integer \(r\) (exact halves round up).
quantityA: The greatest possible value of \(|x - r|\)
solutionStandard: The worst case is when \(x\) sits exactly halfway between two integers (e.g. \(x=2.5, r=3\)), giving \(|x-r|=0.5\) exactly. No value of \(x\) can be rounded with a larger error than this. The quantities are equal.
